availability.p: This edition is licensed under a Creative Commons Licence (available at http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/). You are free: to copy, distribute, display, and perform the work, and to make derivative works under the following conditions: you must attribute the work in the manner specified by the licensor; you may not use this work for commercial purposes; if you alter, transform, or build upon this work, you may distribute the resulting work only under a license identical to this one. For any reuse or distribution, you must make clear to others the license terms of this work. Any of these conditions can be waived if you get permission from the licensor. Your fair use and other rights are in no way affected by the above.

div.head: LECTURE VIII.

head.hi: And he believed in the Lord, and he counted it to him for righteousness.

div.p:
The person to whom the words of my text apply was Abraham. To Abraham it was accounted for righteousness, that he believed in the Lord. What a comfortable and encouraging hope is here held out to all who believe! for, inasmuch as Abraham is called the father of the faithful, it is reasonable to infer, that all who follow him in faith, shall have the portion due to natural posterity, and inherit the blessing of imputed righteousness. And they will inherit so vast a blessing if they believe as Abraham did believe; if, after their faith be tried, it prove glorious; if the trial of their faith present to them on the one hand, the force of custom, the fixed opinion of other men, the influence of example, and even, perhaps, the general laws of what is called nature; on the other, merely the word of God; and under this competition of the flesh and the spirit, they have the grace to be steadfast, and cleave unto the latter.
This sort of trial, in a considerable degree, did Abraham sustain before his faith received the glorious and profitable denomination of righteousness. It was imputed to Abraham as righteousness; not that he assented merely to the wisdom of God, and professed his faith for time to come, but that he had from the first given himself up to be led by the hand of God as an obedient servant: his conduct was the result of faith; faith was not the cover of a lukewarm conduct. I must be allowed to press on your minds this just distinction between a faith which produces good works, and a faith which produces only a good opinion of yourselves; because mistake and confusion on this point have never been without their followers and victims—were never more to be dreaded than at the present moment.

Now that the storms of persecution are past, and that true religion in the sight of God and the Father, might extend itself from sea to sea; many seem to seek their own ruin in an unwarranted construction of Scripture, and to make shipwreck of their souls on rocks that are above the surface. To wrest Scripture from its appointed purpose, to build up systems on some brief insulated passages, and, perhaps, on passages which were themselves but the comments of men on the word of the Lord,
not the word itself, is the very natural consequence of a little learning acting on a proud imagination and uncontrolled passions. To raise up arguments on questions which God has involved in obscurity, as beyond human insight, because unnecessary to human happiness; to draw a presumptuous line of distinction between good works and perfect faith, which our Saviour, by his words and deeds, has pronounced to be inseperably required; to insinuate that the peremptory words of Christ can ever be accompanied to the partial construction of a sect; is the temptation with which Satan, the indefatigable enemy of truth, is now permitted to try the purity of the church.

We are inclosed in on every side by enemies of the Gospel, but by none so dangerous as its pretended friends, who magnify the mercy of God at the expense of his justice, and who might much more profitably employ themselves, if they would always call to mind, that when our Saviour had conducted the inquiring lawyer to the knowledge of good works, he dismissed him with this incontrovertible injunction—"Go and do thou likewise."Luke 10:37 As I before remarked, those who would be very glad to find in a loud declaratio of faith, an excuse for omitting or decrying the weighty matters of commandment, generally shelter themselves under the authority
of some detached texts, and these usually extracted from the Epistles; above all, from Epistles written for particular purposes to persons very differently informed from us, and under many different circumstances peculiar to those times, and which defy all parallel with ours. The words of my text are amongst those often quoted by the extollers of faith. An additional stress also is often laid upon them, because they have been quoted and built upon by St. Paul; out of whose Epistles, voliminous when compared with those of the other Apostles, as preserved to us, with great industry are selected some passages as an authority for doctrines, which, in every instance, the context will be found to condemn, and which he, the Apostle of the Gentiles, again and again reprobates as opposite to the commandment of our Saviour. To such passages, no doubt, St. Peter refers in his 2d Epistle general, when, speaking of the Epistles of St. Paul, he says, "in which are some things hard to be understood, which they that are unlearned and unstable, wrest, as they do also the Scriptures, unto their own destruction."2 Peter 3:16 Hence we may infer, that this dangerous error had crept into the church, even in its very earliest ages; nor shall we wonder at the facility with which it gained admission, when we consider how convenient a doctrine it is for those, who, while they would yet dosomethingsome-
thingto be saved, find it very difficult to give up their darling vices, to keep a constant check upon their passions, and to do unto all men as they would that others should do unto them.

But granting that St.Paul, throughout his numerous Epistles, lays a greater stress upon the necessity of faith, than upon the works of the law; it will very easily be accounted for, if we contemplate the peculiar circumstances which attended both his conversion and ministry. You all know that Paul, or as we has then called, Saul, was originalaly a streneous opposer and violent persecutor of the Christians, till it pleased the Almighty to effect his conversion by a miraculous interposition: from this miraculous interposition we may without presumption conclude, that (his disbelief excepted) Saul was an upright and virtuous man, and that God saw in him a fit instrument to extend, by his zeal and exertions, the knowledge of the truth among the Gentiles: is it then surprising, that the Apostle should most forcibly press upon the attention of his hearers, he knew that hte most punctual fulfilment of the moral law would be unavailing? The difference between us and the Gentiles appears to be ismply this; they might be virtuous, they might be
moral; but St.Paul exhorts them to make perfect their otherwise unprofitable good works by faith. Born in a Christian country, educated by Christian parents, we cannot be supposed to disbelieve, but we are called upon to prove the sincerity of our faith by the consistency of our works.

Again, we may observe, that, although St.Paul dwells with such peculiar energy upon the necessity of faith, he in no instance implies that works are to be omitted; on the contrary, we shall find, in almost all of his Epistles, the most earnest exhortations to the various duties of life. The words of my text are likewise quoted by St.James, who, speaking of the obedience of Abraham, adds, "Seest thou how faith wrought his works, and by works, faith was made perfect?"James 2:22 Let us therefore be assured, that the meaning of these equally inspired writers was the same, though, from their different situations or circumstances, they might find it necessary to dwell most focibly upon different points of doctrine.

I trust I have now satisfactorily proved, that according to common sense, to the obvious construction, or to the construction put on it by St.Paul, the text can in no way be tortured into an argument for faith unconnected
with obedience (which is another word for good works). "Abraham believed in God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness"Genesis 15:6—not in lieu of all obedience, but in pardon of those imperfections which needs must come, when the flesh is not able to do what the spirit willeth. As St.Paul infers in the verse preceding that which quotes our text, that Abraham was not justified by the works of the law, and as the Saviour had not then been crucified for the sins of mankind, it must have followed, that Abraham was without the means of salvation, unless under some previous covenant between God and man. Now, this covenant did exist and had existed from the time when the promise of a Saviour was made in Eden; was the hope of Enoch and all the patriarchs; was the hope which kept them in righteousneess, humanly speaking: it was afterwards renewed with Noah, because he was a just man; and it was the conformity of Abraham's conduct to the terms of this covenant, in other words, it was his obedience to God, and his attention to sacred ordinances, that formed the test of his faith, and so recommended him to mercy, that the righteousness of Christ extended even to him by anticipation. It is therefore of consequence to remember, that the faith of Abraham did not make him a sharer in the benefit of Christ's righteousness, until he had obeyed the voice of the Lord, and seperated
himself from surrounding abominations unto good works for a considerable length of time.

In the history of Abraham, this father and pattern of the faithful, there is nothing of momentary conversion, of sudden illumination, of self-evident election, of infallible assurances of grace: all is gradual, progressive, and consistent. Abraham proceeded from trial to trial from obedience to obedience, and therefore from favour to favour: he proceeded exactly as St.Paul, misrepresented as he is by some, speaks of his own progress, fears, and hopes; "Forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before, I press toward the mark of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus."Philippians 3:13-14

Being assured, then, that our God is not a God of partiality; that in him there is no variableness, neither shadow of turning; we will retrace the clue of his increasing kindness to Abraham, in the uniform obedience of the latter, under each succeeding trial. And if we find that the works of Abraham were done in fear of the Lord, we have no other conclusion to draw than this; to none can faith be ascribed as righteousness, who do not walk in the law of the Lord, mortifying the lusts of the flesh; and of all who hold a contrary doctrine,
David has aptly said, "Their mouth talketh of vanity, and their right hand is a right hand of antiquity."Psalm 144:8

Abraham was seventy-five eyars old, when God first called on him to leave his father's country, and put himself under the care of Providence, for the supply of all his future life. At seventy-five Abraham's character must have been fixed; he must have accustomed himself to walk in the fear or the neglect of God; we have a right to conclude the former, because he is stated to have paid instantaneous obedience to this command of God, which had nothing to recommend it to a worldly man, and which required a sacrifice of feelings implanted by nature. Abraham entered on this appointed pilgrimage, which many might have deemed exile, with alacrity. He persisted in it, spite of all the visitations of incessent fatigue, precarious existence, and occasional famine. He was always warmed with a pious recollection of the true God, and continued in pure worship, uninfected by the idolatries of the surrounding Canaanites. His equanimity, generosity, justice, and strict integrity, have been partly the subject of our preceeding Lectures. In this state of trial Abraham continued for eight years, receiving during that time one or two renewals of God's promise, that unparalled and infinite
blessings were in store for his posterity; but seeing year after year pass by without the blessing of an heir, and of course with diminshed probability of perpetuating his family. Now this in itself was a remarkable trial. How Abraham supported himself under it we cannot be ignorant, when we consider that he did not, under an equivocal construction of God's promise, wish to avail himself of polygamy, then considered under some circumstances venial, but persisted in the safer path of evident purity. He rather chose to doubt his own construction of God's promises, than to wound the peace of her whom he had chosen as the wife of his bosom. That Abraham is entitled to this praise, and that he had himself despaired of offspring, without losing his faith, that in some manner God would justify his truth and fulfil his promise, is clear from the words we read in the 2d verse of the 15th chapter: "And Abram said, Lord God, what wilt thou give me, seeing that I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus?"Genesis 15:2 How plainly do these words bespeak resignation—resignation, and perhaps, disappointment—but no murmuring. "Behold, to me thou hast given no seed; and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir."Genesis 15:3 Abraham does not herein accuse God of any inconsistency; he appears rather to imply that the heir of his substance would be the heir of God's promises.
This humble frame of mind brought its appropriate consolation. Now, God having seen that Abraham was divested of human hope, and yet untouched by ingratitude, speaks to him more decidely than ever: "This shall not be thine heir, but he that shall come out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir: and he believed in the Lord, and he counted it to him for righteousness."Genesis 15:4

Having thus seen that it was no ordinary degree of faith which was ascribed to Abraham for righteousness, before we presume to build any hope thereon that our faith will be similarly ascribed to us for righteousness, we are bound to make our general conduct equal to that of Abraham; nor will that even raise us to a similarity of claim, if we have received stronger evidences of God's mercy than he had. But how very wide is the difference! Abraham had entire faith in promises, which nothing short of miracle could fulfil; we, after all,, put our faith in Him who has fulfilled by miracle all that he promised to Abraham and his seed for ever. He had scarcely one remote speck whereon to rest his spiritual eye. We have a cloud of witnesses which make infedelity as great a reproach to our reason as our piety.

There is, then, no parallel between the faith of those who lived in the promise of a Saviour,
and those who live after his resurrection from the grave. Our faith is indispensible, because facts bear it out, but it will only be attributed to us for righteousness, it will only lead us to justification, by leading us to live according to His law, who is faithful to punish, as well as to save. Christ came into the world to bear witness of the truth; he established truth by his life and death; he gave in his own conduct a proof that perfect holiness only is pleasing to God; so that we have the testimony of Christ, that good works are pleasing to God; not can we pretend to doubt the meaning of his words, when he says, "Let your light shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven."Matthew 5:16—Against all sorts of iniquity, if unrepented of, he denounced exclusion from God's kingdom; so that we have Christ's testimony that the works of the wicked, profess they what they may, are odious to God. And what is the result and union of two such truths but this truth; that those who exercise themselves in the works prescribed by Christ, shall not lose their reward; and that they are encouraged to think their efforts acceptable, through the mediation of Christ? Thus will the faith which leads men to consider their ways, render their imperfect works perfect—thus alone will it be attributed to them for righteousness.
